title: Heat batteries explained: a hot water cylinder alternative?
description: How heat batteries work, what they cost to buy and run compared with a hot water cylinder, and how to pair one with solar or a cheap off-peak tariff.
blurb: Phase change heat batteries store hot water in a third of the space. What they cost, and who they actually suit.
lede: A heat battery does the same job as a hot water cylinder, storing energy now so you have hot water later, but it stores that energy in a melted salt compound instead of a big tank of water. The result is a box around a third of the size of an equivalent cylinder, with lower standing heat losses and no annual safety service. They have moved from niche to mainstream in the UK, mostly in homes with solar panels, heat pumps, or cheap overnight electricity to soak up. The question is whether those gains justify a price two to three times that of a conventional cylinder.
short_answer: A heat battery stores heat in a phase change material rather than hot water, so a unit matching a 210 to 300 litre cylinder takes up roughly a third of the space. Expect around £4,300 to £4,700 installed for a 12 kWh (300 litre equivalent) unit, against £1,500 to £2,500 for a decent unvented cylinder. Charged on a 7p off-peak tariff, a typical household's hot water costs about £102 a year, versus £126 from a gas boiler and £381 on standard-rate electricity. It makes most sense where space is tight or you have solar or cheap overnight electricity to store.

sections.h2: What a heat battery actually is
sections.html:
A heat battery stores energy as heat in a phase change material, usually a non-toxic salt compound similar to the stuff inside a reusable hand warmer. When you charge the battery, electricity (or heat from a boiler or heat pump) melts the salt at around 58°C. When you open a hot tap, cold mains water runs through a heat exchanger inside the unit, the salt re-solidifies, and the heat it releases warms the water on its way to your tap or shower.

The clever part is the physics of melting. A material absorbs a large amount of energy when it changes from solid to liquid without getting any hotter, which is why an ice cube takes so long to melt in a drink. So a phase change material holds three to four times more useful heat per litre than plain water. A unit storing 12 kWh of heat, enough to match a 300 litre cylinder, is roughly the size of a washing machine.

Two distinct product types share the name, so it helps to separate them:

  • Hot water heat batteries replace a hot water cylinder. This is the established category, with several UK manufacturers, and the one this guide focuses on.
  • Space heating heat batteries replace a gas boiler entirely, storing cheap overnight electricity and releasing it into your radiators during the day. These are larger, considerably more expensive, and a different decision altogether, closer in spirit to storage heaters with modern controls.

sections.h2: Heat battery vs hot water cylinder: the practical differences
sections.html:
Against a conventional unvented cylinder, a heat battery has four practical advantages and two real drawbacks.

Where the heat battery wins

  • Size. Around a third of the space for the same hot water delivery. A 150 litre equivalent unit fits in a kitchen cupboard, which is why heat batteries are popular in flats that lost their cylinder to a combi boiler years ago and have nowhere to put one back.
  • Standing losses. The phase change material holds heat well. Sunamp, the largest UK manufacturer, quotes standing losses up to four times lower than a comparable cylinder. Less heat leaking into a cupboard means less electricity or gas wasted keeping water hot you have not used yet, the same problem a cylinder jacket only partly solves on an older tank.
  • No annual G3 service. Unvented cylinders store pressurised hot water and legally need installation and servicing by a G3-qualified engineer, plus discharge pipework to outside. A heat battery stores no pressurised hot water at all, so none of that applies. Mains water is heated on demand as it flows through.
  • Hygiene. Because no water sits warm in a tank, there is no stored-water legionella risk and no need for a weekly anti-legionella heating cycle.

Where the cylinder wins

  • Price. A heat battery costs roughly two to three times as much as an equivalent unvented cylinder, installed. The gap is the main reason cylinders still dominate.
  • Weight and flow. Heat batteries are dense and heavy for their size, so the floor needs to take the load, and flow through the heat exchanger can lag a big unvented cylinder feeding two showers at once. Size the unit to your household rather than buying small.

sections.h2: What it costs to run: the numbers
sections.html:
The running cost depends almost entirely on what you charge it with. A heat battery is just storage; it makes cheap energy available at the wrong time of day useful, but it cannot make expensive energy cheap. The table below shows the annual cost of a typical household's hot water, around 4 kWh of useful heat a day (1,460 kWh a year), by heat source.

How the hot water is heatedRate paidCost per year
Gas boiler (85% efficient)7.33p per kWh£126
Heat battery or immersion, standard rate electricity26.11p per kWh£381
Heat battery charged in an EV-style off-peak window7p to 9p per kWh£102 to £131
Heat battery charged on the cheapest smart tariffsaround 5.5p per kWh£80
Heat battery charged from solar surplus5p to 15p forgone export£73 to £219

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Off-peak examples 5.5p to 9p per kWh, typical EV tariff rates mid 2026. Assumes 4 kWh of useful hot water a day; standing losses excluded.

Three things jump out. First, heating water with standard-rate electricity is the most expensive option by a wide margin, which is why an immersion heater on the daytime rate should always be the backup plan, never the routine. Second, a heat battery charged overnight on a cheap time-of-use tariff beats gas, sometimes comfortably. Third, solar surplus is not free: every kWh you divert into hot water is a kWh you did not export, so its true cost is your export rate. At a 5p export rate diverting is clearly worthwhile; at 15p the case against gas is marginal for the energy itself, though it still beats peak-rate electricity easily.

sections.h2: Pairing it with solar panels or an off-peak tariff
sections.html:
A heat battery earns its keep when it has cheap energy to store, and the two common sources are solar PV and off-peak electricity.

With solar PV

If you have solar panels, a heat battery does the same job as an immersion-and-cylinder setup fed by a solar diverter: it catches midday surplus generation and turns it into evening hot water. The heat battery version simply wastes less of it overnight thanks to the lower standing losses, and fits where a cylinder will not. A typical 4 kW array generating 3,400 to 4,000 kWh a year produces far more summer surplus than a household's hot water needs, so from roughly April to September your hot water can be close to free apart from the forgone export payment.

It is worth being clear about what a heat battery is not. It is not a substitute for electrical battery storage. A lithium battery can run your fridge, lights and kettle in the evening; a heat battery can only ever give the energy back as heat. Plenty of solar homes end up with both: the electrical battery for general load shifting, the heat battery for hot water.

With an off-peak tariff

No solar needed. If you have, or could get, a tariff with a cheap overnight window, the battery charges at 5.5p to 9p per kWh while you sleep and delivers hot water all day. As the table above shows, that undercuts gas, and it works in homes that cannot have panels at all, such as north-facing roofs and many flats. If you are weighing up panels anyway, the sums in whether solar is worth it change in solar's favour once a heat battery or diverter lets you use surplus generation rather than export it for pennies.

As of mid 2026, a 12 kWh hot water heat battery, the size that matches a 300 litre cylinder, typically costs £4,300 to £4,700 installed. Smaller units equivalent to 150 or 210 litre cylinders cost less, and installation is usually a single day because there is no discharge pipework and no G3 paperwork. A comparable unvented cylinder installed is more like £1,500 to £2,500, so you are paying a premium of £2,000 or more for the compactness and efficiency.

The VAT position is messier than for most energy-saving kit. Heat batteries installed as part of a heat pump system have qualified for the 0% VAT rate on energy-saving materials, while a standalone heat battery retrofit has in some cases attracted the full 20%, and the industry has been lobbying to close that gap. The rules have shifted more than once, so check the current HMRC energy-saving materials guidance on gov.uk before you sign a quote, and ask the installer to itemise the VAT treatment.

There is no standalone grant for a hot water heat battery, but if you are replacing a fossil fuel boiler with a heat pump, the hot water store (cylinder or heat battery) is part of the funded system under the Boiler Upgrade Scheme. Scheme amounts and eligibility change, so confirm the current figures on the official gov.uk page before planning around them.

sections.h2: The bottom line: who should buy one
sections.html:
A heat battery is the better hot water store in almost every technical respect: smaller, better insulated, no servicing burden, no stored-water hygiene worries. What it is not, yet, is the cheaper one. If you have a working cylinder and floor space to spare, replacing it with a heat battery purely to save on standing losses will not pay back; the losses on a well-insulated modern cylinder are worth perhaps £50 to £100 a year even at electric rates.

The strong cases are these:

  • You have no room for a cylinder. Flats and small homes wanting solar hot water, a heat pump, or simply to escape an ageing combi. This is the killer application: the heat battery fits where nothing else does.
  • You have solar PV and nowhere to put the surplus. A heat battery plus diverter turns 5p export into hot water that would otherwise cost 26.11p to make at peak rate or 8 to 9p on gas.
  • You have a cheap overnight tariff. Hot water at 5.5 to 9p per kWh beats gas without a boiler in sight, useful for all-electric homes that would otherwise pay £381 a year heating water at the standard rate.
  • You are installing a heat pump and space is tight. Heat-pump-ready heat batteries exist, and buying the store as part of the heat pump installation is also when the VAT and grant treatment is most favourable.

If none of those describes your home, a conventional cylinder with a decent jacket and lagged pipes remains the sensible, boring answer, and the money saved buys a lot of insulation.

faq.q: Is a heat battery the same as a home storage battery?
faq.a: No. An electrical storage battery stores electricity you can use for anything in the house. A heat battery stores energy as heat in a phase change material and can only give it back as hot water (or space heating, in the larger models). Heat batteries are cheaper per kWh stored and do not degrade like lithium cells, but the energy is locked into one use.

faq.q: How long does the heat last in a heat battery?
faq.a: Days rather than hours. The phase change material is well insulated and manufacturers quote standing losses several times lower than an equivalent hot water cylinder, so heat stored from cheap overnight electricity or midday solar is still there the following evening with only a small loss.

faq.q: Does a heat battery need servicing?
faq.a: No annual service is required. Unlike an unvented cylinder, a heat battery stores no pressurised hot water, so the G3 regulations, discharge pipework and yearly safety checks that apply to cylinders do not apply. The phase change material itself is sealed and designed to last for tens of thousands of charge cycles.

faq.q: Can a heat battery work with a gas boiler?
faq.a: Yes. Most hot water heat batteries can be charged by a boiler, a heat pump, an immersion-style electric element, solar PV surplus, or a combination. In practice the financial case is strongest when charged with cheap off-peak electricity or solar surplus, because gas can already heat water cheaply on demand.

faq.q: How much does a heat battery cost in the UK?
faq.a: As of mid 2026, around £4,300 to £4,700 installed for a 12 kWh unit equivalent to a 300 litre cylinder, with smaller 150 and 210 litre equivalent units costing less. That is roughly two to three times the installed cost of a comparable unvented cylinder, so the premium buys space saving and lower heat losses rather than a quick payback.
